Term Paper · Machine Learning

From Bag-of-Words to Parameter-Efficient Transformers

A controlled comparison of 22 models for Arabic sentiment analysis on the ASTD corpus — classical machine learning, deep learning, and MARBERT + LoRA, evaluated on equal footing.

Rayyan Tawfieg  ·  Dept. of Electrical & Computer Engineering, Birzeit University
01 Motivation

Why is Arabic sentiment analysis so hard?

Morphology

A three-letter root, many faces

Prefixes, suffixes, and vowel changes spin one root into dozens of surface forms. Count-based models treat each variant as a brand-new word — sparsity explodes.

Dialect

One word, different meanings

The word لبن (laban) means milk in Egyptian Arabic but yogurt in Levantine. A model trained on one dialect doesn't reliably generalise to another.

Data

Small, rare, imbalanced

Arabic is a minority language online. Labelled sentiment datasets like ASTD are small — and heavily skewed toward one class.

02 The Dataset

ASTD: the Arabic Sentiment Tweets Dataset

9,684 tweets after cleaning, each labelled positive, negative, neutral, or objective. Under Schema A, we merge neutral + objective.

  • 75.1% of tweets are NEUTRAL
  • 17.0% are NEGATIVE
  • Only 7.9% are POSITIVE

Always predicting the majority class scores 75% accuracy — a meaningless number. That's why we use macro F1 as our primary metric throughout the paper.

Fig. ASTD class distribution — three quarters neutral, positive is the rarest class.
03 Research Questions

Four questions, one fixed protocol

RQ1

Does how we handle the dominant objective class change performance?

RQ2

Does augmentation (SMOTE, back-translation) beat simple stemming or label smoothing?

RQ3

Does MARBERT + LoRA outperform classical and deep-learning models?

RQ4

Is parameter-efficient LoRA effective on a small corpus with limited resources?

04 Experimental Design

One protocol, four label schemas

Stratified 60/20/20 split · seed 42 · macro F1 primary metric · every extractor fit on train only.

SchemaDefinitionClassesTest N
AOBJ merged into NEUTRAL31,939
BOBJ dropped entirely3645
COriginal four-class41,939
DFour-class, OBJ downsampled to 1,5004945

Two preprocessing pipelines: an aggressive classical pipeline (stemming, stop-word removal) vs. a light transformer pipeline that keeps stop words for MARBERT's WordPiece tokenizer.

05 Methodology

End-to-end experimental pipeline

ASTD → 4 label schemas → 2 preprocessing pipelines → 3 model families → shared metric harness. Split and seed held constant throughout.
06 Models

22 configurations across three families

Classical

Naive Bayes · Random Forest · Linear SVM

TF-IDF (10k terms), Word2Vec, and FastText representations. SVM also tested with stemming, SMOTE, and back-translation.

Deep Learning

CNN · RNN · LSTM

Trainable 100-d embeddings over a 10k vocabulary, 50-token inputs, Adam optimiser with early stopping.

Transformer

MARBERT + LoRA

163.4M-parameter Arabic tweet-pretrained transformer, adapted with low-rank matrices at r = 16 and r = 32.

Fig. Low-rank adaptation: frozen pretrained weights + trainable rank-r matrices A, B.
07 Parameter Efficiency

Fine-tuning 163M parameters on a laptop

LoRA freezes the pretrained MARBERT weights and injects a pair of small trainable matrices per layer:

W = W₀ + (α/r)·BA   ,   r ≪ min(d,k)
  • Only 592k of 163.4M parameters trained — under 1%
  • Feasible on a 16 GB Apple M1 Pro, no CUDA, no fp16
  • Avoids overfitting a small corpus with a huge model
08 Results

Macro F1 across all models

All MARBERT variants (blue) sit above every classical and deep-learning model on the comparable 3-class task.
Best overall: MARBERT LoRA r=16 + LS — 0.650
Best classical: Stemmed SVM — 0.507
Best deep learning: CNN — 0.455
09 RQ1

Does the label schema matter?

SVM across schemas
MARBERT across schemas
  • Four-class Schema C is hardest for both SVM and MARBERT
  • Downsampling (Schema D) recovers much of the loss: SVM 0.410→0.463, MARBERT 0.531→0.618
  • Dropping OBJ (Schema B) gives MARBERT its highest raw score, 0.669 — but on a smaller, cleaner test set
10 RQ2

Does augmentation help minority classes?

SVM: stemming vs. SMOTE vs. back-translation
MARBERT progression across interventions
  • Stemming is the one classical lever that works: SVM 0.481 → 0.507
  • SMOTE hurts — it creates noisy minority samples in high-dimensional space
  • Back-translation moves the needle by a quarter of a point — not worth it
  • Label smoothing lifts MARBERT (0.626→0.650); adding focal loss + higher rank + augmentation together does not
11 RQ3 & RQ4

MARBERT + LoRA: the clear winner

65%
MARBERT + LoRA + LS macro F1
50%
Best classical (stemmed SVM)
46%
Vanilla CNN

LoRA trains just 592k of 163.4M parameters — under 1% — yet still produces the best results of all 22 configurations. Raising rank from 16 to 32 did not help, though that run also added focal loss and augmentation at the same time.

12 Efficiency

Quality doesn't come cheap

Accuracy vs. memory footprint (log scale, bubble ∝ params)
ModelMemoryTrainInfer/1k
SVM (stemmed)<1 MB<1 s0.12 ms
MARBERT+LoRA650+ MB~690 s~7.5 s
14% higher score costs
2,700× memory
1,070× train time
62,000× inference latency
13 Error Analysis

Same accuracy, very different mistakes

Row-normalised confusion matrices for all models
  • Classical models collapse toward NEUTRAL, almost never predicting POSITIVE
  • Deep-learning models over-predict minorities when uncertain
  • MARBERT is the most balanced across all three classes — the root cause of its higher macro F1 at similar accuracy
14 Discussion & Limitations

What we learned — and what we didn't cover

Three takeaways

  • In-domain dialectal pretraining wins — MARBERT's 1B-tweet pretraining is decisive
  • Better loss + stemming beat augmentation
  • Efficiency trade-offs matter for production decisions

Limitations

  • Schemas differ in size/classes — comparisons are indicative, not absolute
  • ASTD skews Levantine/Egyptian; Gulf and Moroccan underrepresented
  • 16 GB cap limited full fine-tuning of MARBERT
  • Single seed, no significance testing across close results
15 Conclusion

Choose your model by your constraint

Accuracy-bound

Use MARBERT + LoRA

>60% macro F1 while training under 0.4% of its parameters — feasible on common 16 GB hardware.

Latency / memory-bound

Use the stemmed linear SVM

Cheap, fast, and comparably strong on cost-to-performance ratio.

Future work: more dialect coverage, multiple random seeds, and full fine-tuning on less-constrained hardware.
